revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Kk]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />

The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />

{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />

The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />

In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Tamagushi]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Other websites ==
* [https://archive.today/20230630012635/https://hirutabutsuguten.shop/?pid=136761056 Image of Masasaki] * [http://www.t-carry.com/image/syouhin/shinden/masakakiA_L.jpg Masakaki] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070319154252/http://www.t-carry.com/image/syouhin/shinden/masakakiA_L.jpg |date=2007-03-19 }} * [http://www.yoshihira.co.jp/sakaeru/sougon.html Masakaki installation example]

In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Tamagushi]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Tamagushi]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Other websites ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11583274]] Wikidata name: masakaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Tamagushi]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Other websites ==
* [https://archive.today/20230630012635/https://hirutabutsuguten.shop/?pid=136761056 Image of Masasaki] * [http://www.t-carry.com/image/syouhin/shinden/masakakiA_L.jpg Masakaki] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070319154252/http://www.t-carry.com/image/syouhin/shinden/masakakiA_L.jpg |date=2007-03-19 }} * [http://www.yoshihira.co.jp/sakaeru/sougon.html Masakaki installation example]
{{shinto shrines}}

[[Category:Shinto religious objects]]
}}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Masakaki}} [[ja:真榊]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Masakaki]] [[zh:真榊]]
[[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{wikidata link|Q11583274}}

The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11583274]] Wikidata name: masakaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11583274]] Wikidata name: masakaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
* [[Tamagushi]]
== References == {{reflist}}
== Other websites ==

Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Sakaki]]. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]].<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

The five colors of the cloth stand for the [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|five elements]]. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, [[:en:Shinzo Abe|Shinzo Abe]] got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, [[:en:Fumio Kishida|Fumio Kishida]] ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11583274]] Wikidata name: masakaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Sakaki}}. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=five elements}}. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

The five colors of the cloth stand for the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=five elements}}. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]]
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Sakaki}}. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=five elements}}. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida|1=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[sn:" to "[[Category:sn_crud|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Masakaki]][[en:Masakaki]][[ja:真榊]][[simple:Masakaki]][[zh:真榊]]
{{Short description|Shinto ritual object}}
{{Multiple image | image1 = Masakaki with Sword.jpg | alt1 = Left Masakaki at Yasaka Shrine featuring a sword | caption1 = Left Masakaki featuring a sword | image2 = Masakaki with Mirror and Jewel.jpg | alt2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | caption2 = Right Masakaki featuring a Mirror and Magatama | footer = Masakaki at [[Yasaka Shrine]] }} '''Masakaki''' (真榊) is an object used in [[Shinto]] rituals.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Masakaki">{{Cite web |title=What is a Masakaki Offering? |url=https://www.jiaponline.org/2016/10/what-is-masakaki-offering.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Sakaki-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-09-21 |title=Sakaki {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |access-date=2023-11-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210921215257/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9610 |archive-date=2021-09-21 }}</ref> It is put on both sides of a table where the event takes place. Masakaki is made with branches of a tree called {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}}. These branches are attached to the top of colorful cloth banners. The banners are in five colors - green, yellow, red, white, and blue.<ref name="d-2015a" /><ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
They were mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]] as implements used to get [[Amaterasu]] to leave the cave.<ref name="Sakaki-2021" />
Special treasures are hung on these branches. On the left side, a sword is hung. On the right side, a mirror and a [[magatama]] are hung. The three of them symbolize the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.<ref name="d-2015a" /> Sometimes, people call the whole setup with a special name, Masakaki-dai. A masakaki offering involves donating two such Masakaki to a shrine or [[Jichinsai|construction ceremony]] with the three treasures.<ref name="Masakaki" />
The five colors of the cloth stand for the {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=five elements|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)}}. These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida|1=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>

These elements are wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. At funerals, Masakaki with yellow and white banners is used.<ref name="d-2015a">{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2015-04-05 |title=The Colours of Shinto (masakaki) |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2015/04/05/the-colours-of-shinto/ |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
In 2016, {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzo Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} got controversy for making a Masakaki offering to {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-10-17 |title=Abe sends ritual offering to Yasukuni Shrine |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2016/10/17/national/politics-diplomacy/abe-sends-ritual-offering-yasukuni-shrine-name-prime-minister/ |access-date=2023-11-30 |website=The Japan Times |language=en}}</ref> It was incorrectly reported as being an entire tree by some sources.<ref name="Masakaki" /> In early 2023, {{ill|Fumio Kishida|en|Fumio Kishida|WD=Q359480|lt=Fumio Kishida|1=Fumio Kishida}} ran into a similar controversy.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://apnews.com/article/japan-yasukuni-kishida-offering-militarism-china-korea-0a875a9251a8e1cc488fbfa6a8a3c251 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=AP News |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kishida donates spring offering to contentious shrine in Tokyo {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14890805 |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-04-21 |title=Japan PM sends offerings to controversial Tokyo shrine |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/china-ap-fumio-kishida-yasukuni-shrine-tokyo-b2324414.html |access-date=2023-11-27 |website=The Independent |language=en}}</ref>
== See also ==
